coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of graphene quantum dots from defective CVD graphene and their application in IGZO UV thin film phototransistor
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Huihui
Liu, Ao
Shan, Fukai
Yang, Wenrong
Zhang, Wenling
Li, Da
Liu, Jingquan
coredata.dc:description: Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) has recently been considered as the most reliable method to prepare high-quality monolayer graphene films, yet the as-grown graphene usually contains wrinkles and cracks or suffers from discontinuity. These defects can easily result in the shredding of large-sized graphene into small pieces even under a gentle disturbance. Herein, this work presents a cost-effective new method to produce high-quality GQDs by vigorous sonication of defective CVD graphene. The prepared GQDs can be easily and stably dispersed in organic solvents. Morphology and optical properties of the GQDs are investigated using a number of techniques. And we observed the as-prepared GQDs are highly homogeneous, mostly consisted of single-layered graphene, roughly round shapes less than 8 nm in a diameter, and exhibited a strong blue luminescence. Impressively, it is also confirmed that the as-obtained GQDs can act as a promising light absorption material for phototransistor with a hybrid film of GQDs and indium gallium zinc oxide (IGZO) as the channel layer. The GQD/IGZO phototransistor exhibited an appreciated photocurrent, which is 10 times larger than that of the IGZO one when exposed to 270 nm light.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51173087 and 51472130 ) and Qingdao ( 12-1-4-2-2-jch ), and Taishan Scholars Program for financial support. H. H. Zhu and A. Liu contributed equally to this work. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of GQDs using defective CVD graphene sheets. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) SEM image of graphene on Cu foil. (b) Raman spectrum of graphene transferred on SiO2. (c) The spatial distribution of sheet resistances and (d) The transmittance spectrum for graphene sheet on glass. Insets in (b) and (d) show the corresponding photographs of graphene on SiO2 and glass, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) HRTEM image of GQDs. (b) High magnification image of selected area in red box in (a). (c) Size distribution obtained from TEM measurements (300 samples). (d) HRTEM image of GQDs showing zigzag edge and (e) The corresponding 2D FFT images. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a, b) AFM image and height profile of GQDs. (c) Height distribution obtained from AFM measurements (300 samples). (d) XPS and (e) Raman data for GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV/Vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs in dichloromethane. Inset: Photograph of the GQD solution taken under visible light and 365 nm UV irradiation. (b) Electronic transition mechanism of triple carbenes at zigzag sites. (c) PL spectra of the GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (d) Photostability test of the GQD solution under irradiation with Xe light (375 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The variation of transfer curves of the IGZO phototransistors exposed to dichloromethane. Characteristics of the thin-film phototransistors in dark and under illumination (270 nm ≤ λ ≤ 400 nm), (b) IGZO, (c) GQD/IGZO bi-layer. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of graphene quantum dots from defective CVD graphene and their application in IGZO UV thin film phototransistor

College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) has recently been considered as the most reliable method to prepare high-quality monolayer graphene films, yet the as-grown graphene usually contains wrinkles and cracks or suffers from discontinuity. These defects can easily result in the shredding of large-sized graphene into small pieces even under a gentle disturbance. Herein, this work presents a cost-effective new method to produce high-quality GQDs by vigorous sonication of defective CVD graphene. The prepared GQDs can be easily and stably dispersed in organic solvents. Morphology and optical properties of the GQDs are investigated using a number of techniques. And we observed the as-prepared GQDs are highly homogeneous, mostly consisted of single-layered graphene, roughly round shapes less than 8 nm in a diameter, and exhibited a strong blue luminescence. Impressively, it is also confirmed that the as-obtained GQDs can act as a promising light absorption material for phototransistor with a hybrid film of GQDs and indium gallium zinc oxide (IGZO) as the channel layer. The GQD/IGZO phototransistor exhibited an appreciated photocurrent, which is 10 times larger than that of the IGZO one when exposed to 270 nm light.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a single atomic monolayer of sp 2-bonded hexagonal carbon, is a rapidly rising star on the horizon of materials science and has triggered intensive research activities worldwide because of its remarkable electrical, mechanical, and thermal properties [1–4]. One of the most studied and tantalizing directions in graphene research is to use it as the functional material for electronic devices. For example, graphene-based transistors have been developed and now considered as one of the important alternatives for post-silicon electronics [5]. However, large-area graphene is a zero-band gap semimetal material with its valance and conduction bands cone-shaped meeting at the K points of the Brillouin zone [6]. Therefore, it is impossible for the devices with channels made of large-area graphene to be switched off and, hence, are not suitable for logic applications. Nevertheless, theoretical and experimental works revealed that the band structure of graphene can be tuned [7]. Up to now, it is possible to open a band gap in graphene through several methods, for instance, biasing bilayer graphene [8], applying strain to graphene [9], constraining large-area graphene in one dimension to form graphene nanoribbons [10], and forming “small graphene pieces”, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a hot newcomer of graphene family [11,12].

GQD, a quasi-zero-dimensional graphene material, has great potential applications in next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices due to quantum confinement and edge effects [6,13–15]. In brief, graphene exhibits an infinite exciton Bohr radius, for bulk graphene has a zero band gap and charge carriers have zero effective masses at band edges, thus quantum confinement could take effect in graphene of any finite size and is expected to result in some exotic phenomena and remarkable properties that can rarely be obtained in other semiconductors [6]. In addition, the edge structures are demonstrated to be mainly responsible for the inducement of the strong luminescence of GQDs, for electronic transitions between the highest occupied molecular orbits (HOMOs) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbits (LOMOs) directly generate UV/Vis absorptions and photoluminescence (PL) [11]. Recently, the GQDs were mainly synthesized from graphene oxides by chemical exfoliation under strongly acidic environment [11], lithographic methods including masking, pattering and lift-off [16], electrochemical scissoring of graphene sheets [15] and bottom up synthesis by wet chemistry [17]. However, these methods require strong oxidants and/or nasty acids, and/or time-consuming multistep processes, which hinder the efficient synthesis of high-quality GQDs for practical applications [18]. The lack of GQDs stability in dispersion agents is also a drawback for various electronic and energy applications.

Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is the most reliable method to prepare high-quality monolayer graphene films, yet the as-grown graphene usually contains wrinkles and cracks or suffers from discontinuity [19–21]. The formation of wrinkles is attributed to differences in the thermal expansion coefficients between graphene or graphite with respect to copper (αgraphene = −6 × 10−6/K at 27 °C [22]; αa-graphite = 0.9 × 10−6/K between 600 and 800 °C [23]; αCu = 24 × 10−6/K [23]). Besides wrinkles, a number of reports revealed that cracks can also be observed in graphene grown on Cu foil. It seems that it is a common and almost unavoidable issue to bring some defects and cracks into graphene films during the graphene growth, etch and transfer processes [24,25]. These defects can easily result in the shredding of large-area graphene into small pieces even under a gentle disturbance. In addition, the exfoliation and dissection effects on carbon materials via sonication treatment have been reported previously. Smalley and co-workers obtained individual nanotubes with length ranging from 80 to 200 nm by ultrasonically agitating an aqueous dispersion of raw single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) in sodium dodecyl sulfate [26]. Blackburn's and Haddon's groups further demonstrated the effective exfoliation of SWNTs via sonication [27,28].

Herein, we describe a cost-effective new method based on vigorous treatment of defective CVD graphene with a sonicator to produce high-quality GQDs (Fig. 1 ), which could be easily and stably dispersed in an organic solvent. Compared with previous reports, the GQDs in this work exhibit intact structure [29], uniform size and thickness [30], and strong and stable PL intensity [15], as demonstrated by TEM, AFM, Raman, PL spectra, etc. We also confirm that the as-obtained GQDs could act as a promising light absorption material for phototransistor with a hybrid film of GQDs and indium gallium zinc oxide (IGZO) as the channel layer. The addition of gallium can suppress oxygen vacancy formation, which is beneficial to relieve the persistent photoconductivity phenomenon [31]. In addition, the water induced method employed in this study for the preparation of IGZO channel layer is more suitable for the fabrication of low cost and functional electronics [32–34] in comparison with the sputtering method for the fabrication of IGZO/graphene dots phototransistor [35]. Our GQD/IGZO phototransistor exhibited an appreciated photocurrent which is 10 times larger than that of the IGZO one when exposed to 270 nm light.

First, large-area monolayer graphene was grown on 25 μm thick copper foils (3 × 3 cm2, Alfa Aesar, item No. 13382) using a CVD method developed by Ruoff and co-wokers [36]. Briefly, a copper foil was inserted into a quartz tube of 100 mm in diameter with a 4 standard-state cubic centimeter per minute (SCCM) H2 flow and then annealed for 30 min at 1000 °C. A 40 SCCM CH4 flow was subsequently controlled for graphene growth and the total pressure was maintained at 750 mTorr for 30 min. Then, the furnace was cooled down to room temperature under H2 conditions (2 SCCM, 50 mTorr). The back side of graphene on the Cu foil was exposed to O2 plasma (3 min, 100 W) for exfoliation. Then as-obtained Cu foil was etched in ammonium persulphate solution with a concentration of 0.1 M, leaving graphene floating on the surface of the etchant. The resultant solution was sonicated with ultrasonic cell disruptor (BILON92-IID, 400 W) for 10 min (see more details on sonication power and time in Supporting Information). After that, the solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove large graphene sheets, and then diluted with dichloromethane (1/5, V/V). The extraction was then performed with a separating funnel.

The thin-film phototransistors were made on a bottom-gate p-type Si substrate with a thermally grown 100 nm SiO2 dielectric layer on the top. The IGZO precursor solution was prepared by using water-inducement method developed in our lab [32–34]. The indium nitrate hydrate, gallium nitrate hydrate, and zinc nitrate hexahydrate were dissolved into de-ionized water. The molar concentrations of indium, gallium, zinc precursors were controlled at 0.085 M, 0.0125 M, and 0.0275 M, respectively. The IGZO precursor solution was filtered through 0.2 μm syringe filter and then spun on SiO2/Si substrates at 5000 rpm for 30 s. After that, the samples were annealed at 300 °C for 4 h in air. Finally, Al source and drain electrodes, with a thickness of 100 nm, were deposited on channel layers by thermal evaporation. The channel length and width were 250 and 1000 μm, respectively. For the fabrication of GQD/IGZO thin-film phototransistor, the prepared GQDs sample solution was dropped onto the exposed IGZO channel with a micropipette, and the device was then set in ambient air enabling evaporation of the dichloromethane. The electrical characteristics of the devices were measured under ambient conditions using a semiconductor parameter analyzer (Keithley 2634B) in the dark box.

Fig. 1 is the schematic illustration of the synthesis of GQDs using defective CVD graphene sheets. The motivation for the preparation of GQDs using defective graphene sheets based on the following two considerations: 1) Defect-rich CVD graphene can be easily smashed into pieces due to the existence of stress concentration [22,37]; 2) Vigorous sonication facilitates the smash process [38]. As shown in Fig. 2 a, scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of the as-obtained CVD graphene on Cu revealed the presence of discontinuous graphene flakes, also wrinkles and steps in these flakes. The isolated flakes may result from insufficient carbon source supply while the wrinkles are associated with the thermal expansion coefficient difference between Cu and graphene. Wang et al. found that a copper surface reconstruction and the evolution of surface faceting are observed during the cooling process inside the environmental scanning electron microscope [25]. Notably, the reconstruction underneath the carbon sheets is distinct and can easily be differentiated from the reconstruction of uncovered copper, as shown in atomic electron microscopy (AFM) image as well (Fig. S1). Raman spectrum is an unambiguous method to evaluate the quality and the number of graphene layers [24]. Fig. 2b shows the Raman spectrum of defective monolayer graphene, including a 2D-band with a full width half-maximum (FWHM) of ∼30 cm−1 located at ∼2662 cm−1, a G-band at 1582 cm−1, and a D-band, evidencing the defects in graphene, at 1331 cm−1. The intensity ratio of the 2D band to G band (I2D/IG) is about 2. The sheet resistance of the graphene film transferred onto a 2 × 2 cm2 glass (shown in Fig. 2c) exhibited an average value of 14.7 ± 4.8 kΩ/square, which is larger than those reported by others [36,39], also implying the presence of discontinuity and defects of the graphene film. Fig. 2d depicts the high optical transparency of the graphene (∼97.54% at 550 nm). All the above results demonstrated that the graphene sheets were single-layered, discontinuous and defective.

The as-obtained GQDs were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and AFM. Fig. 3 a shows HRTEM image of GQDs. For comparison, TEM image and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of pristine CVD graphene are shown in Fig. S2. We found the sizes of these monodispersed GQDs were uneven due to the occurrence of radial fragmentation from each defect part, that is, stress concentrative point [40]. In Fig. 3b, the HRTEM image showed the high crystallinity of the GQDs and a lattice parameter of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon, revealing that the GQDs kept the similar crystallinity with graphene [41]. Fig. 3c gives the size distribution, where three main distribution areas are illustrated, ranging from 2 nm to 8 nm. Because of the small size, the zigzag edge structure of these GQDs is more evident (Fig. 3d), and well corresponds to their 2D fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns (Fig. 3e) which also confirmed the esthetic hexagonal honeycomb structure with the bond lengths of 1.42 Å for C–C/CC. It has been established by Radovic et al. that the free zigzag sites are carbene-like, with a triplet ground state being most common [42].

The AFM image (Fig. 4 a, b and c) show that the majority fraction (81%) was made up of 0.5–0.6 nm thick GQDs, and no GQDs with height of more than 1 nm was found, indicating that the most GQDs were single-layer graphene, a result which is superior to most of those previous reported [43,44]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to determine the composition of the GQDs. In Fig. 4d, as expected, the GQDs showed a typical peak at 284.5 eV derived from sp2 carbon with negligible oxygenous peaks, which is similar to that of graphite without oxygenous functional groups [25,44]. The result confirmed that the sonication method developed here only caused ignorable oxidation of GQDs. In Raman spectroscopy study, Fig. 4e showed a disordered (D) band at 1358 cm−1 and a crystalline (G) band at 1600 cm−1, and the intensity ratio of the D to the G band (ID/IG) is merely 0.826, which not only indicates the high quality of these GQDs, but also the uniqueness of the sonication and extraction method developed here for GQDs preparation.

The edges and shapes of the obtained GQDs above might be responsible for their optical property and electronic structure. Theoretically, carbenes have two electronic configurations in ground states: singlet and triplet. For the singlet state described as σ2, two nonbonding electrons are paired in the σ orbital with the π orbital being vacant, while for the triplet state σ1π1, the two orbitals σ and π are singly occupied [11]. The carbene ground-state multiplicity is related to energy difference (δE) between the σ and π orbitals, which has been determined to be less than 1.5 eV for a triplet ground state by Hoffmann [45]. Because the triplet state is the most common one at zigzag edges [42], we may ascribe the two visible absorption bands in the UV/Vis spectrum (Fig. 5 a) to the electronic transitions from the σ and π orbitals (highest occupied molecular orbitals, HOMOs) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs), respectively. The electronic transition mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 5b, from which the δE was calculated to be 1.28 eV, meeting the requirement for triplet carbenes, also suggesting the assignment above is reasonable. In addition, bright blue fluorescence was visually observed when the GQD solution was irradiated with 365 nm UV light (inset in Fig. 5a), which is one of the unique optical properties of GQDs compared to large-sized graphene. PL analysis (Fig. 5c) indicated that the strongest emission of the GQDs occurred at 435 nm with a small Stokes shift of 65 nm (0.46 eV). The three-peak curvilinear shape is presumably related to the uneven size effect of these GQDs, similar to that of different-sized intrinsic GQDs derived from graphite nanoparticles as reported by Seo's group [44]. As shown in Fig. S3 and S4, the optimal quantum yield of the as-prepared GQDs was found to be 22.6% using quinine sulfate as the reference (see more details in Supporting Information). According to Table S1, our value is much higher than most others. Over one month, no PL bleaching was observed, as shown in Fig. 5d, indicating that these GQDs could be dispersed in solvent stably. The optical properties of these GQDs are well consistent with other defect-free colloidal GQDs [25,44,46].

The diverse applications of GQDs in photovoltaics, light emitting diodes, photodetectors and other electronic devices have been reported recently [14,21,47]. In light of GQDs' excellent photoresponse performance, for the first time we faricated a new thin-film phototransistors based on GQD/IGZO hybrid structure. The amorphous IGZO TFT is a leading candidate for the next generation of large-area electronics systems [48], as it can be deposited at low temperatures with high uniformity over large areas, showing high mobility and high transparency. It is well known that the electrical characteristics of oxide TFTs are sensitive to the environmental changes around the channel [49]. Therefore, the solvent molecules adsorbed on the channel surface could accumulate extra electrons due to its polarity or electronegativity. In this regard, we used dichloromethane as dispersing agent for GQDs owning to its less polarity and high volatility. Note that the IGZO phototransistor treated by dichloromethane exhibited negligible changes in the transfer curve (Fig. 6a), which indicated that dichloromethane is an appropriate dispersion agent for GQDs in our study.

The transfer characteristics of the devices measured in dark and under illumination for IGZO phototransistor without and with GQDs at several wavelengths are shown in Fig. 6 b and c. The light source is a 250 W Xe lamp equipped with a monochromator for spectral response measurement. As shown in Fig. 6b, upon light illumination (λ = 400–270 nm), the IGZO phototransistor showed the turn-on voltage (V on) shift within 2.5 V regardless the wavelength illuminated. This shift might be resulted from the light-induced instability of the IGZO phototransistor. The increased off current (I off) at short wavelength is likely due to the photo-generated excess carriers in the IGZO thin film [50]. In contrast, for the IGZO phototransistor with GQDs (phototransistor mode), the V on shifted negatively from 0 V (dark) to −20 V (λ = 270 nm). The on current (I on) with light exposure at a gate voltage of 30 V was found to be 10 times larger than that in dark. The significant increase in I on should be due to the electron transfer from GQDs to the underlying IGZO to establish a photochannel [35], which correspondingly enhance the light-detecting ability of the phototransistor. In addition, the calculated photo-dark current ratio (sensitivity) at various gate voltages indicated that the phototransistor with GQDs exhibited higher detecting sensitivity than that of the original IGZO phototransistor (see Fig. S5 in the Supporting Information). The sensitivity of GQD/IGZO phototransistor reached approximately 106 at 0 V gate voltage under 270 nm light illumination, which is 3 orders of magnitude higher than that of IGZO phototransistor.

In summary, we have developed a novel and facile approach for preparation of GQDs from defective CVD graphene via sonication and extraction processes. The as-prepared GQDs are highly homogeneous, mostly consisted of single-layered graphene, roughly round shapes less than 8 nm in a diameter, and exhibited a strong blue luminescence. We have also revealed that the blue luminescence is dominated by intrinsic structure in the high-crystalline structure other than oxygenous functional groups, since these GQDs showed much lower oxidation extent with the triplet carbene in the zigzag edge. Furthermore, as a promising light absorption material, the as-prepared GQDs were integrated into a metal-oxide thin-film phototransistor using solution process for the first time, which exhibited enhanced photocurrent in sensing UV/Vis lights. The photocurrent of the GQD/IGZO phototransistor was found to be 10 times larger than that of IGZO one. We expected the as-prepared could be used for the fabrication of cost-effective and highly-sensitive electronics in the future application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51173087 and 51472130) and Qingdao (12-1-4-2-2-jch), and Taishan Scholars Program for financial support. H. H. Zhu and A. Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

Supplementary data associated with this article including Characterizations, GQD quantum yield measurements, Fig. S1-S5 and Table S1 can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.01.016.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.01.016.
